reviews.author: syzbot ci
reviews.summary: The reviewer reported a WARNING in preempt_count_sub due to a lock ordering violation, where vswap_free() acquires the per-vswap spinlock while holding the folio lock, and requested that the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap().
reviews.raw_body:
syzbot ci has tested the following series

[v1] mm: reliable 1GB page allocation
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260520150018.2491267-1-riel@surriel.com
* [RFC PATCH 01/40] mm: page_alloc: replace pageblock_flags bitmap with struct pageblock_data
* [RFC PATCH 02/40] mm: page_alloc: per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator
* [RFC PATCH 03/40] mm: page_alloc: split-path PCP free with local-trylock + remote-llist
* [RFC PATCH 04/40] mm: mm_init: fix zone assignment for pages in unavailable ranges
* [RFC PATCH 05/40] mm: page_alloc: remove watermark boost mechanism
* [RFC PATCH 06/40] mm: page_alloc: async evacuation of stolen movable pageblocks
* [RFC PATCH 07/40] mm: page_alloc: track actual page contents in pageblock flags
* [RFC PATCH 08/40] mm: page_alloc: superpageblock metadata for 1GB anti-fragmentation
* [RFC PATCH 09/40] mm: page_alloc: support superpageblock resize for memory hotplug
* [RFC PATCH 10/40] mm: page_alloc: add superpageblock fullness lists for allocation steering
* [RFC PATCH 11/40] mm: page_alloc: steer pageblock stealing to tainted superpageblocks
* [RFC PATCH 12/40] mm: page_alloc: steer movable allocations to fullest clean superpageblocks
* [RFC PATCH 13/40] mm: page_alloc: extract claim_whole_block from try_to_claim_block
* [RFC PATCH 14/40] mm: page_alloc: add per-superpageblock free lists
* [RFC PATCH 15/40] mm: page_alloc: add background superpageblock defragmentation worker
* [RFC PATCH 16/40] mm: compaction: walk per-superpageblock free lists for migration targets
* [RFC PATCH 17/40] mm: page_alloc: superpageblock-aware contiguous and higher order allocation
* [RFC PATCH 18/40] mm: page_alloc: prevent atomic allocations from tainting clean SPBs
* [RFC PATCH 19/40] mm: page_alloc: aggressively pack non-movable allocs in tainted SPBs on large systems
* [RFC PATCH 20/40] mm: page_alloc: prefer reclaim over tainting clean superpageblocks
* [RFC PATCH 21/40] mm: page_alloc: adopt partial pageblocks from tainted superpageblocks
* [RFC PATCH 22/40] mm: page_alloc: add CONFIG_DEBUG_VM sanity checks for SPB counters
* [RFC PATCH 23/40] mm: page_alloc: targeted evacuation and dynamic reserves for tainted SPBs
* [RFC PATCH 24/40] mm: page_alloc: prevent UNMOVABLE/RECLAIMABLE mixing in pageblocks
* [RFC PATCH 25/40] mm: trigger deferred SPB evac when atomic allocs would taint a clean SPB
* [RFC PATCH 26/40] mm: page_alloc: refuse fragmenting fallback for callers with cheap fallback
* [RFC PATCH 27/40] mm: page_alloc: cross-migratetype buddy borrow within tainted SPBs
* [RFC PATCH 28/40] mm: page_alloc: drive slab shrink from SPB anti-fragmentation pressure
* [RFC PATCH 29/40] mm: page_reporting: walk per-superpageblock free lists
* [RFC PATCH 30/40] mm: show_mem: collect migratetype letters from per-superpageblock lists
* [RFC PATCH 31/40] mm: page_alloc: per-(zone, order, mt) PASS_1 hint cache
* [RFC PATCH 32/40] mm: debug: prevent infinite recursion in dump_page() with CMA
* [RFC PATCH 33/40] PM: hibernate: walk per-superpageblock free lists in mark_free_pages
* [RFC PATCH 34/40] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable
* [RFC PATCH 35/40] mm: page_alloc: refuse best-effort high-order allocs servable at lower orders
* [RFC PATCH 36/40] mm: page_alloc: set ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT on alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof
* [RFC PATCH 37/40] mm: page_alloc: move spb_get_category and spb_tainted_reserve to mmzone.h
* [RFC PATCH 38/40] mm: compaction: skip empty tainted superpageblocks as migration source
* [RFC PATCH 39/40] mm: compaction: respect tainted SPB reserve in destination selection
* [RFC PATCH 40/40] mm: page_alloc: SPB tracepoint instrumentation [DO-NOT-MERGE]

and found the following issue:
WARNING in preempt_count_sub

Full report is available here:
https://ci.syzbot.org/series/8eef71e1-708c-40d0-9f9d-3a1bd637fc80

***

WARNING in preempt_count_sub

URL:       https://kernel.googlesource.com/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/akpm/mm.git
base:      4b83cbc4c15f09b000cc06f033f64b0824b6dc87

compiler:  Debian clang version 21.1.8 (++20251221033036+2078da43e25a-1~exp1~20251221153213.50), Debian LLD 21.1.8

------------[ cut here ]------------

RBP: ffff888121042dc0 R08: ffffffff903129c3 R09: 1ffffffff2062538

CR2: ffff88823ffff000 CR3: 000000000e74a000 CR4: 00000000000006f0
Call Trace:
 <TASK>

***

If these findings have caused you to resend the series or submit a
separate fix, please add the following tag to your commit message:
  Tested-by: syzbot@syzkaller.appspotmail.com

---
This report is generated by a bot. It may contain errors.
syzbot ci engineers can be reached at syzkaller@googlegroups.com.

To test a patch for this bug, please reply with `#syz test`
(should be on a separate line).

The patch should be attached to the email.
Note: arguments like custom git repos and branches are not supported.

2026-05-21.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the allocation of large memory blocks by dividing memory into superpageblocks and steering unmovable, reclaimable, and high-atomic allocations into those already tainted by such allocations. The goal is to leave as many 1GB superpageblocks available for movable allocations, which can be easily defragmented for regular or huge pages. Various strategies are employed to achieve this, including preferential allocation from tainted blocks, fallback to small pages for certain kernel allocations, and migration of movable memory when a block falls below a free threshold. This approach enables more extensive use of 2MB THPs and mTHPs, as well as reliable allocation of 1GB pages. The series is built on top of Johannes's PCPBuddy series, which aims to reduce the need for CPUs to get pages from zone free lists by relying on page block ownership.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Usama Arif questioned the performance implications of kernel allocations falling back to small pages, specifically asking if this would result in a performance impact and whether it would also affect 2M THP allocation efficiency due to increased fragmentation of kernel memory.

Reviewer questioned whether the patch's strategy for handling movable allocation failures would result in an Out-Of-Memory (OOM) condition.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Rik!

The comments are just based on coverletter.

Hopefully will get to review all the patches. The above one of
kernel allocations falling back to small pages is interesting.

- Will it result in a performance impact as kernel allocations
wont benefit from higher order allocation?
- Will this impact 2M THP allocation efficiency due to more
fragmentation of kernel memory?

---

by allowed, do you mean if movable allocations fail, it will
result in OOM?

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledges that the current solution may not be sufficient and suggests exploring an alternative approach to limit the number of 1GB blocks being spilled over into simultaneously, particularly when partially used for kernel memory.

Author acknowledges that the increased availability of clean 1GB page blocks will make THP (Transparent Huge Pages) allocation easier due to more movable memory being available.

The author is addressing a concern about the potential for reserved-for-unmovable memory areas to exceed the zone low watermark, leading to early OOMs. The author acknowledges this as a bad situation and suggests that protection against it would be needed if it were to happen.
reviews.raw_body:
It might! We may well need a better solution
here, like spilling over earlier, but limiting
the number of 1GB blocks we can spill over into
simultaneously (partially used for kernel memory).

---

THPs come from movable memory. With more 1GB
page blocks not having kernel allocations in
it, THP allocations should be easier.

---

Yes, but by that time the zone free memory should
also be below the low watermark, because there 
should only be a few partially occupied tainted
1GB page blocks.

If the zone has 300MB low watermark, and there
are 50MB tied up in those reserved-for-unmovable
memory areas, it should not cause early OOMs.

I don't know if we can end up in a situation
where somehow the reserved-for-unmovable memory
would add up to more than the zone low watermark.

That would be bad, and if it happened we would
have to add some sort of protection against that.


-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-05-22.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the allocation of large memory blocks by dividing memory into superpageblocks and steering unmovable, reclaimable, and high-atomic allocations into those already tainted by such allocations. The goal is to leave as many 1GB superpageblocks available for movable allocations, which can be easily defragmented for regular or huge pages. Various strategies are employed to achieve this, including preferential allocation from tainted blocks, fallback to small pages for certain kernel allocations, and migration of movable memory when a block falls below a free threshold. This approach enables more extensive use of 2MB THPs and mTHPs, as well as reliable allocation of 1GB pages. The series is built on top of Johannes's PCPBuddy series, which aims to reduce the need for CPUs to get pages from zone free lists by relying on page block ownership.

reviews.author: David Sterba
reviews.summary: Reviewer David Sterba agreed that GFP flags could be improved for self-documentation and suggested cleaning up the patch's booleans in a future iteration, allowing the 40-patch series to proceed.
reviews.raw_body:
Agreed, the GFP flags would be better and also a bit more self
documenting where the functions are called. The patch is not standalone
so I don't mind having the booleans, we can clean it up later to let the
40-patch series proceed.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author agrees that the GFP mask should be passed in by each caller and plans to resend a cleaned-up patch, indicating a fix is planned.
reviews.raw_body:
I think this patch could go in by itself. It doesn't
depend on anything else in the series.

I sent it with this series mostly because it's needed
to make the series work, and to provide context on why
it's needed.

I'm happy to resend it with a GFP mask passed in by
each caller. That would look better, indeed!

I'll send you guys a cleaned up patch this coming week.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-05-23.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the allocation of large memory blocks by dividing memory into superpageblocks and steering unmovable, reclaimable, and high-atomic allocations into those already tainted by such allocations. The goal is to leave as many 1GB superpageblocks available for movable allocations, which can be easily defragmented for regular or huge pages. Various strategies are employed to achieve this, including preferential allocation from tainted blocks, fallback to small pages for certain kernel allocations, and migration of movable memory when a block falls below a free threshold. This approach enables more extensive use of 2MB THPs and mTHPs, as well as reliable allocation of 1GB pages. The series is built on top of Johannes's PCPBuddy series, which aims to reduce the need for CPUs to get pages from zone free lists by relying on page block ownership.

reviews.author: Matthew Wilcox
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that the use of GFP_NOFS in the patch is a bad code smell, as it requires deep filesystem knowledge to understand where memalloc_nofs_save() should start. The reviewer suggested lifting the GFP_NOFS usage to the callers of this function and passing in GFP_NOFS | __GFP_MOVABLE (or plain GFP_NOFS) instead.
reviews.raw_body:
If you're respinning anyway ...

Every use of GFP_NOFS is a bad code smell.  Filesystems should be using
memalloc_nofs_save/restore.  The problem is that it takes deep filesystem
knowledge to understand where the memalloc_nofs_save() should start.
It would be good to lift the GFP_NOFS to the callers of this function as
it gets us slightly closer to being able to replace it with GFP_KERNEL
in the places it's not actually needed.

ie what I'm asking for is, please don't do the 'extra_gfp' thing.
Just pass in gfp from the callers and make them all pass in GFP_NOFS |
__GFP_MOVABLE (or a plain GFP_NOFS) for now.  Someone can come along
later and change them to GFP_KERNEL later where warranted.

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the changes to GFP flags can be bubbled up a layer, making it easier to manage and improving the situation, but manual intervention will still be required for full correctness.
reviews.raw_body:
Note that is should be pretty trivial to just bubble it up a layer
for changes like this.  This will naturally gravitate to more useful
places.  To make it fully correct it will need manual intervention,
but generally bubbling up should improve the situation.

Same for GFP_NOIO.

The goal is to leave as many 1GB superpageblocks available for movable allocations, which can be easily defragmented for regular or huge pages. Various strategies are employed to achieve this, including preferential allocation from tainted blocks, fallback to small pages for certain kernel allocations, and migration of movable memory when a block falls below a free threshold. This approach enables more extensive use of 2MB THPs and mTHPs, as well as reliable allocation of 1GB pages. reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that removing /proc/sys/vm/watermark_boost_factor without replacement or deprecation notice breaks userspace, and requested adding Documentation/ABI/removed/ note and keeping sysctl entry as no-op for a few cycles

Reviewer noted that the patch's changes cause the 'boost' field in /proc/zoneinfo and show_mem output to disappear, suggesting a similar fix is needed.
reviews.raw_body:
This is a userspace-visible removal. Writes to
/proc/sys/vm/watermark_boost_factor will now return -ENOENT instead of being
accepted, breaking userspace.

I am not sure whats been done in the past, but we need to add a
Documentation/ABI/removed/ note explaining what replaces it (SPB steering).
and also maybe keep the sysctl entry as a no-op with warning for a few
cycles before deletion?

---

The boost: field in /proc/zoneinfo and the show_mem output also disappears.
Something similar might need to be done for this as well.

2026-05-26.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the allocation of large memory blocks by dividing memory into superpageblocks and steering unmovable, reclaimable, and high-atomic allocations into those already tainted by such allocations. reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch is no longer needed and decided to drop it due to potential harm caused by watermark boosting, agreeing to remove it from the series.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a fix is not needed
reviews.raw_body:
I'll just drop this patch for now.

While we no longer need it, and the watermark boosting
can cause a little bit of harm at times, this probably
isn't worth the extra code churn in this series.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.
